variants.title: Snakes smell the air with their tongues
variants.source_license: Derived analytics brief; new media required before render
variants.description: Watch the tongue smell behind this animal behavior: Snakes smell the air with their tongues
variants.seo_title: Snakes smell the air with their tongues
variants.yt_description: Snakes smell the air with their tongues. Watch the tongue smell first, then replay the opening clue.
variants.hook: Snakes sample the air with a tongue flick.
variants.script: Snakes sample the air with a tongue flick. Watch the tongue flick, because it collects scent particles and sends them to a special organ in the mouth. That helps snakes track prey, mates, and danger without needing a loud chase. The air is information. Would you notice the trail?

variants.title: Cats groom to reset their scent map
variants.source_license: Derived analytics brief; new media required before render
variants.description: Watch the grooming signal behind this animal behavior: Cats use whiskers like built-in rulers
variants.seo_title: Cats groom to reset their scent map
variants.yt_description: Cats groom to reset their scent map. Watch the grooming signal first, then replay the opening clue.
variants.hook: Cats use grooming to reset their scent map.
variants.script: Cats use grooming to reset their scent map. Watch the tongue and paw movements, because grooming spreads familiar scent across the coat. variants.title: Tigers wear stripe shadows as camouflage
variants.source_license: Derived analytics brief; new media required before render
variants.description: Watch the stripe camouflage behind this animal behavior: Tigers wear stripe shadows as camouflage
variants.seo_title: Tigers wear stripe shadows as camouflage
variants.yt_description: Tigers wear stripe shadows as camouflage. Watch the stripe camouflage first, then replay the opening clue.
variants.hook: Tigers use stripes to break up their outline.
variants.script: Tigers use stripes to break up their outline. Watch the stripes against grass and shade, because they split the body outline into harder pieces to track. That camouflage helps a big animal get closer before prey locks on. The pattern is stealth, not decoration. Would you spot the tiger first?

variants.seo_title: Trees record wet years inside their rings
variants.yt_description: Trees record wet years inside their rings. Watch the tree rings first, then replay the opening clue.
variants.hook: Trees keep weather history in their rings.
variants.script: Trees keep weather history in their rings. Watch the trunk pattern, because each growing season leaves a new layer. Wide rings often mean easier growth, while thin rings can mark stress. That is why an old tree can become a climate diary. Which hidden record should come next?

variants.seo_title: Monkeys wash food when grit gets annoying
variants.yt_description: Monkeys wash food when grit gets annoying. Watch the learned habit first, then replay the opening clue.
variants.hook: Monkeys can change habits to solve small problems.
variants.script: Monkeys can change habits to solve small problems. Watch the hands near the food, because some monkeys learn useful routines from their group. variants.source_license: Derived analytics brief; new media required before render
variants.description: Watch the 3d vision behind this animal behavior: Mantises judge distance with 3D vision
variants.yt_description: Mantises judge distance with 3D vision. Watch the 3d vision first, then replay the opening clue.
variants.hook: Mantises can measure a strike in 3D.
